summary: My father immigrated from Germany when he was about 16 years old. His father was German and his mother was English. The family eventually settled in Massachusetts, where I grew up.
My mother came from a farming family in Kansas. She married my dad while he was in the Air Force. Their roots from Kansas were part of my family background.

We settled in Massachusetts because that was where my dad's family was based. I grew up in a suburban town there, similar to what Bellevue, Washington was like before it grew. I also attended the University of Massachusetts for my sociology degree.

I grew up in a suburban town much like Bellevue, Washington, back in the day before it got huge. It was mostly a white town with many upwardly mobile families, and minorities were not really present. This shaped my early experiences.

I was the first in my generation to go to college and I attended the University of Massachusetts where I studied sociology. After graduating, I got married and eventually moved to Seattle with my husband for his graduate studies. My time there laid the foundation for my later career decisions.

My husband and I moved to Seattle in 1971 so he could pursue his PhD in sociology. I initially worked in a factory there, but after seeing the tough labor conditions and layoffs at Boeing, I decided to go back to school for nursing. Seattle became the place where my nursing and union journey really began.

When we arrived in Seattle in 1971, Boeing had just laid off 50,000 people, which was a huge blow to the city. I worked in a factory soldering projectors and noticed the lack of union representation on the assembly line. This experience sparked my early interest in labor organizing.

I started working for Group Health after graduating nursing school in 1981 and loved the idea of it. It was at Group Health where I became heavily involved in union organizing, including the 39-day strike in 1989. The union we formed there made a huge difference in my life as a single mother and in improving working conditions.

Back in 1975, the WSNA was the only union representing nurses and they called a citywide strike that was very poorly organized and ultimately a disaster. That experience made me realize we needed a real, member-led union rather than the WSNA, which was led by managers and academics. Although WSNA has improved since then, at the time they were not effective as a bargaining unit.

Actually, the citywide strike I recall was in 1975, not 1983, and it was led by the WSNA. It was a terrible strike where no one won anything and many nurses lost their positions or were bumped to less desirable shifts. This failure motivated me to help form a new, stronger union for nurses.

At one point, I was so frustrated with the WSNA's failure that I even considered whether we should join the Teamsters. I was open to any union that would truly represent nurses and improve our working conditions. However, we eventually found a better fit with 1199 and SEIU.

When I started my nursing career, I remember a checker at Safeway across the street made more money than I did as a professional nurse. Safeway workers were on strike that summer for higher wages and had a union, the UFCW. This comparison highlighted how undervalued nurses were at the time.

The UFCW was the union representing Safeway workers who were on strike the summer I started nursing. Their ability to strike for better wages and actually make more than nurses showed me the power of unionizing. It was a motivating factor in our own organizing efforts.

Compared to Swedish Medical Center and Virginia Mason, we had better healthcare benefits at Group Health, including zero cost for premiums and copays. This was a point the employer used to argue against our demands, but we still pushed for more improvements. It showed the competitive environment among Seattle hospitals.

Virginia Mason was another hospital in Seattle where nurses had less favorable healthcare benefits compared to Group Health. We often compared conditions and benefits with places like Virginia Mason to highlight the need for improvements. It helped us argue for better staffing and pay.

The 39-day strike at Group Health in 1989 was a pivotal moment for us. We were frustrated with the bargaining process and demanded changes like the Low Census Fund, which protected our pay when patient numbers were low. Winning that strike brought huge improvements in job security and wages for nurses.

We looked to 1199 New York as a model for a member-led, democratic, and progressive union. Eventually, we joined the part of 1199 that affiliated with SEIU. This union's focus on civil rights, equity, and inclusion matched our values and helped us build a strong organizing effort at Group Health.

After joining 1199, we became part of SEIU, which aligned with our goals for social justice and strong member leadership. SEIU's structure helped us unify different worker groups in the institution under one local. Being part of SEIU strengthened our bargaining power and organizing capacity.

Glenn Goldstein was an incredible organizer we brought in from New York. He worked tirelessly, probably 20 hours a day, talking to anyone and everyone. His storytelling about union successes in New York inspired many of us and helped build momentum for our organizing and eventual strike.

Group Health eventually became part of Kaiser Permanente. Since then, we've had fewer long strikes, maybe about five picket lines total. The union continues to advocate for better staffing and working conditions under Kaiser, building on the foundation we laid at Group Health.
